title: O Adelín
text: O Adelín by Michael Wehunt W e formed a broken circle around the fire. In the empty space sat a black glass stone, proxy for the blood man. Its surface flickered oily red. The night was too warm for fire but we desired its light and its invitation to commune closer than most would. Eleven of us aged mercenaries had set out over the little mountains bound for El Porvenir and six remained. We sipped sour mash from tin cups. Nearby the horses trembled and showed their teeth. For luck we had yet to name them. The cart groaned under the weight of bison skins, among them the bounty heads of a rogue capitán and two of his men. The low drone of flies stitched the air. Wind soughed through the stand of ash trees in which we made our camp and all eyes were averted from the strange stars glittering down at us. The moon had shrunk from a crescent rind into nothing for days. We kept our weapons near to hand. Our voices were scant murmurs punctuated by the ruecrows roosting black in the trees calling out their omens. The city lay some five miles distant on the cracked desert below the mountains. It looked like something a man could pinch between two fingers and rub against a boot heel. Mud houses atop clay streets and the sun yet hours from rising into its kiln. He who would finish the circle sat removed upon a lip of the ridge. That blood man. A mottled horsehide draped about his frame and he was the only one hatless among us. His long legs were tucked beneath his lap, his eye inscrutable in the moonlight. He had but the one eye and no name he shared with the likes of us. Dull company he was. But we needed him near. # The mountains had been no more than rough sentinels sharpening out of the dim distance toward us when we lost our first rider in the scrubland. Old Podrel the whisperer, whose vanishing made me the eldest of us. There had been drunken argument regarding the stars that night as we eleven settled around an old well. Cassie had quit her chair in the firmament, claimed Podrel, and Orion lofted not the lion’s pelt but hung instead from the beast’s mouth. Great Oso had trundled around the earth from our sight. Most of us held no truck with the heavens, I among them, yet soon we all would come to confess that the stars did appear changed in the spread sky, and diminished as well, as though retreating from their forsaken watchers. It was when the next morning Podrel’s blanket was empty that my heart began to soften against such foolishness. The land we crossed was still low, the trees few. An easier country than the Mexican desert that waited beyond the mountains. The old man couldn’t have wandered far. We set out in twos, wary of the once-peaceful Apache tribes who though slaughtered for years by the whites were still known to lash out at us darker cousins. But the day brought nothing but night and weariness and we camped to wait on the sun. Julo disappeared while we slept and his brother Ramiro was taken the following night, whether by desire to find his kin or by our pursuer we did not know. Our cook, Lupe the Younger, before the fourth dawn. Finally the leader of our band, steelfaced Mantalbo with his rifles a great X upon his back, took his contempt of our growing unease into the dark and did not return. We all read the stars after that. Their cold portents glared clearer. We bound ourselves together when we slept until after the blood man came. We were one day into the mountains when William spied him up a redoak tree staring down at us, his eye dripping blood into the fire. Soro was the first to stab up into the branches, but the man receded into the greater reach of the foliage, skittering backward up the trunk like some pale spider. An hour found us far from that tree and huddled together in the dark risking neither fire nor sound. Our wrists were again bound together. We’d have bet our purses that yonder was the thing taking us one by one in the nights. But at first light we saw that he’d slithered from the redoak and scented our trail. He reposed beside me and that black stone was his pillow. We fell upon him linked as we were like a chain of paper dolls, limbs tangled. When the shouts rang off into silence he smiled. “You do not see the stars?” he said, lying uninjured at his rest upon the ground, the one eye a milky yellow and beside it a ruined hole scooped of its meat. “They have taken your companions. I too seek El Porvenir. You will arrive there whole.” We were six and the blood man traveled alongside with his oath of safe passage. And though we neither trusted nor counted him among us, it was in his presence that we held our number. # “I still think he’s got Mantalbo and them stashed somewhere,” Sorrén, the youngest left of us, said. He cocked a thumb at the hatless man who sat gazing upon the city. “Probably ate them.” “Maybe so,” William said. His orange hair shone like flames escaping the coals. “I wish he’d conjure up a rabbit or two. Jerky’s giving me mouth sores.” The rest of us said nothing to court attention from that lone figure. Someone spat into the fire and another chased after it with a stick, prodding the embers into a spray of sparks. Each looked into the turning flames and prayed to his mode of Creation. “What are you in mind of, Gris?” Huedro said after a time. He looked at me and soon the others followed suit. “I’m not in mind of much,” I said. It was a lie but it served to pry open my curiosity. “Fact is that man needs talking to.” I had to will myself over to his perch, clearing my throat like a nervous young suitor. “The men reckon you’re a wizard,” I said. The words seemed to die in the air on their way to him. The blood man stared out across the desert and bade his silence. Two of his long fingers crept out from under the hide, plucked a tick or a clod of dirt from his lap. “We’re all thankful to still be around come sunrise,” I said after a minute, “but time is we learn—” “The men reckon I am a wizard.” He did not turn as he cut into my words, his voice flat as his polished stone or the desert under his scrutiny. “What is your reckoning, Guenard?” I stepped back and my creaky legs nearly spilled me. He’d used my birthname when no man had since as a young man the first silver threaded my beard like early ore. “I reckon you know things,” I told him. The blood man sat unmoving as an effigy and watched the night. At length his hand appeared again and swept across the land. “One would think El Porvenir thrives in its dust, so unaltered it appears.” “I’m glad of it myself.” A string of caws from wheeling crows seemed to scold me for the sentiment. That they’d become nocturnal was strange as the vanished moon. “We’ve come far to get our payday, so it’s good that it has our coin.” “It is no longer a city. The currency there you perhaps will not recognize.” “Another thing we reckon is you’re the one who carried our friends off. Man like you seems wily enough. Strong enough too.” “No. You see the changed stars. And those among them see you.” With that the blood man rose. He turned and that sightless black cave regarded me deeper than his good eye ever could. His face was carved with lines and valleys and puckered scars. A short covering of dark hair, like a monk from the far south, and no beard or even the shadow of one. He passed into the dark like oilsmoke. His stone remained by the fire. The others looked to me but I shook my head. Quiet drew down. The horses calmed and the crows sought, we hoped, bleaker scenes. After some talk we elected to forgo lashing ourselves together. The blood man had our trust, thin as it was. # My worn bladder had grown small the last of those sixty-eight years and carried blood in it. On those nights when we joined our wrists, the others made me relieve myself before we all lay down in a heap. I’d chew wiltgrass to ease my discomfort. But that night I was unbound. The pain built to a gnaw and I awoke staring up at that infinite dome and its dwindling points of light. Like the earth waited to be swallowed into a great mouth of black. The Dippers had slunk across the sky and hung in the middle of it like watchful eyes beneath an insect’s antennae. It turned bitter cold. The shivers wouldn’t quit and I walked off a proper ways past the firelight. Unbuckling myself took longer than it had any business to. I braced a hand against the bark of a stunted tree and pissed as my bones shook in that dark with the crackles of the snoring men and the dying fire behind and nothing ahead. But those sounds fell away and the night folded around me. “Guenard,” something whispered, and the lilt it rode was that of my wife who had borne me four strong daughters. My Isa who lay with the eating sickness a hundred miles west in another world altogether. The stream of urine shut off with a fierce burning pinch and I gasped. Her voice drifted out at the edge of my ears, saying I had enough gold for her doctors and lacked only the comfort of her bosom and her fingers trailing through my hair, which she would soon wash in the warm brook coursing deeper in the wood. She waited to lull me with the song of the lady Adelín who cleansed the world with the tears of her lovers, to which we’d swayed at our wedding. Obscured in the fog of my brain I knew it was the blood man, for he had spoken my true name just hours before. But my wife knew that song well, and what passed for my soul was already singing it with her. Breath plumed from my mouth. I buttoned my trousers and stepped forward, the rest of my urine running hot down my leg. Leaves rustled above though the wind had long ceased. “I’m in the trees,” Isa whispered, and a hand fell on my shoulder. Its fingers were long enough to clamp over my breast and the heart that lay knocking beneath it. “Easy here, swordsman,” the blood man said into my ear. He pulled me back and only my helpless moan continued into the dark. I turned and could only stare at my hand, which had lifted to strike him. “Get your tricks off me, wizard.” I waited for my heart to unclench. A knot of thunder gathered in it. The blood man wore a faint nimbus of light. He removed his hand and laid it upon my chest. The pain loosened itself at once. “Now you know what befell your companions and why I cannot sleep.” The skin around his socket flexed and the good eye had regained its milky yolk color. “What speaks as my wife?” He watched me from the unknowable, godless face. In his other hand he clutched the black stone. “Those who have long peered through keyholes. Eyes that have studied the mites crawling along the skin of the world and the motes shifting in its sun.” “How is the city changed?” I asked him. The questions at my tongue were many and I seized at the one that sat in the distance upon our road. Talk of stars was beyond my reach. “I told you it is no city. It now has a key fitted into it.” “We aim to keep on.” I could speak for the six of us. With Mantalbo gone the governor of El Porvenir might think to break our contract, though we’d sewn up our end with the three dead men whose heads we carted. But there was bison to sell at the least. My Isa was sick, and we had children with children, a nest of mouths to feed. “Build up the fire, Guenard. Keep out of the night.” I did as the blood man told and he sat and watched us in our uneasy rest. # We came down out of the mountains and rode into the desert, six wayward pilgrims in that vast waste and the blood man riding behind. He clung bareback to Julo’s spotted horse as the sun clawed up from the earth’s rim to blacken us upon the cracked pan. El Porvenir awaited our return to Mexico, wavering in the distant haze. We hoped to reach it before midday. Our bellies were full and sloshing with water, the horses renewed and content to leave our terrors behind in those low peaks. The wagon clattered on bent wheels. Five miles. The sun a white blur in the center of everything and yet the dry ache of the desert could not do its work to our bones in such a length. We rode at an easy clip and the worry dropped away from our faces. Soro and William, side by side, discussed the shapes and colors of the whores they would buy in the city, while Huedro called ahead to remind them they had plump young wives keeping their beds warm. Lupe the Older, he who was now mere Lupe, rode next to me and we each sat in the quiet of our own minds. But far too early the land drained of its heat and the horizon to the west bled an array of inks. We squinted ahead through the whirling grit and saw that the lines of El Porvenir had changed. Something huge and rounded, like a mountain peak made blunt, pushed up above the buildings. “Blood man,” Huedro called as Julo’s horse drew near, “what does this mean?” We drank from canteens and watered the horses from our hats. The blood man stared across those miles at the city and his eye socket seemed to dilate. After a minute he answered. “It means you wait until dawn, whatever dawn may be. Mark me now. I’m the only of us who must go into that place.” He kicked his horse forward and the hide he wore flapped at his back like a lost flag. The sun slipped meekly away and a pure black began to clog each pore of the sky save for a dense cluster of stars near the bowl’s center. I pictured a plughole opened and the celestial wash cluttered at its mouth. Under this unnatural constellation we debated each to himself. I had never heard such profound quiet. It seemed to eat the creak of leather and the snorts of the animals. “Do we ride on?” William asked. He looked like snow in the darkness against the chestnut of his mount. “Don’t much like the thought of bedding down here.” “Gather a fire,” I answered, for the last light had all but fled. There was little material for it, so we collected the dryer, topmost bison hides. Soon a greasy smoke clouded our meager camp, the stench from the burning skins heavy and close. The horses clomped off to the edge of the firelight. A supper of dried meat was passed around. We wondered aloud if the blood man hadn’t deceived us and circled back into the mountains. For if fear had mastered that strange man then what hope had we from this unknown? My eyes never strayed long from those stars converged in their basin. Whether the remainder of that mute endless sky was empty of all things or muddied with approaching menace, none could say. The whole of it quivered with meaning and we shrank to nothing more than dust in its lidless eye. Lupe took the first watch while the rest of us wrapped ourselves in hides and curled up in that void, lashed together once more. The city had fallen into it for all we knew. Only the same blackness lay in its direction and even that great hump was obscured. Huedro was the first. I’d taken second watch and I saw him open his eyes and with his teeth pull apart the rope binding his wrist to Sorrén’s. He stood and let the firelight flicker across his face for a moment before turning away. “Coraza?” he whispered toward the dark and then walked off. It was the name of his mother who was said to be passing fair. “Huedro!” I hissed his name again and again but he was lost, absorbed into a place I would not go. After Soro cut his bonds and wandered off into the same dark, I roused the others, cursing my frail self. There was no more sleep. # Dawn came and in it El Porvenir gave the illusion of having crawled through the small hours toward us. The earthen dome seemed to gather the city into itself, although its apex had now broken or caved in from some structural flaw. The horses hadn’t moved in the night. We rode out trailing the two riderless animals behind us. The sky had emptied of all life and the hardpan was dotted with black carcasses of fallen ruecrows and vultures. We passed inside the low walls of the city onto streets shrouded in a silent pall. A hearsewagon sat full of the dead in front of an unmarked adobe building. As we watched an old white woman crept from beneath the canvas cover. She sat in the clay street and slowly pulled out her eyes on their stalks. More bodies shuffled within the wagon, the canvas bulging, and we nudged our horses on. Any taste for good whiskey had withered in our guts. An empty cantina slid by and there civilization ended, for soon we came to the immense mound. Twenty or more feet high, the thing was surely crafted upon some god’s potter wheel, still glistening as though newly sculpted. From within a wide hole near its base issued a throng of voices in murmurs and halting laughter, unhindered by the collapse of the mound’s roof. The air carried a moist tang. Embedded in the soft mud of the structure were the naked waists and legs of figures twitching like worms after a hard rain. The blood man’s bare horse lay dead in the gray light. “What now?” William said. “Looks like the governor ain’t in.” “The others could be in there,” I said, but I was thinking of Isa, for I’d picked her voice out among the soft din. “And I need gold to pay for any years my wife has left to her.” “Well, I ain’t going in that hole,” Sorrén said even as he sat gazing into it. His eyes were wet and he’d lost his hat somewhere. “They got my son’s baby girl,” Lupe whispered. “I hear her fussing.” The rest of what he said collapsed into a sob and we watched him climb down from his horse and approach the mound. Not one of us spoke a word as Lupe bent over and slipped into the darkness of the opening. As if his movement had tipped the axle of the world itself, night began to fill the city. The three left in our procession looked straight up and there nearer than ever were the stars packed into a miasma. They looked an arm’s reach away, and I didn’t lower my eyes as William too crawled into the hole. “Gris, you coming?” Still I kept my eyes in the heavens. “Reckon so, Sorrén,” I said. It was awful to hear my own voice or anything but Isa singing of Adelín and all those vaqueros along the train of her dress. There were a great many other sounds and voices but they only served as the pulse of percussion. Then Sorrén was gone and I sat numb on my horse. The other animals folded themselves down to the earth. I ruminated on keyholes and the spaces between stars. Isa sang to me and I felt my blood pulling in my veins like iron filings to a magnet. In the last red streaks of sun I saw the blood man’s stone lying beside his horse. I dismounted and walked into the rising chorus of song—voices piling onto Isa’s now—and hefted it in my hand. Silence sheared through all and devastated me. I watched the naked men wriggling into the surface of the mound. I laid my other hand on the slick wall and felt something hum through every piece of me before that too was sucked up into the stone. My horse nudged me on the shoulder and I fell back casting my eyes around that dead city. A crowd of folk gathered in the dark street. Nude forms with distended bellies and their eyeless faces tilted up toward me. In the near distance I saw others scuttle to meet them, dragging their vulgar pregnancies in the dirt. I turned back in witness of a far greater arrival. Behind the mound a shadow swelled in the night. The earth trembled with the immense weight of something both walking and crawling toward the town. The clump of stars was blotted out as a vast black thing stood in front of them and unfurled itself. In my distraction the townsfolk had closed upon me. I pulled my blade and cut down something that might have once been the city governor. His hanging belly strained in a dying attempt at birth. The pale woman from the hearsewagon clasped her arms around my legs. Another of the nude figures grappled with my sword, its fingers coming off against the blade. Soon I was backed onto the narrow boardwalk before the cantina. The great shadow filled yet more of the sky and I sensed something reaching down toward me. I saw my horse in the distance and broke for it, pulling myself up into the saddle. “Isa love, forgive me,” I said with a look back at the mound, its mouth vague and muted by the blood man’s stone, which I still held inside my shirt against my skin. I blessed the horse for not abandoning me, then turned and rode north out of El Porvenir at a gallop, striking at those still in my path. # The mountains proved as hard to reach as the city had the day before. Their dim peaks were like old friends and though I foolishly rode through the night they seemed to draw little closer. The strange sounds of this new world remained muted so long as the stone touched my flesh. An hour before dawn the horse went down with a scream and though I fell and rolled with all my old ease, the stone was flung out of my shirt. It skittered across the parched hardpan and in an instant I knew I had been followed. My eldest daughter was in my ear. “It’s Mama’s time,” Benicia said. Her voice was cracked and the cracks were filled with sorrow. “She’s calling for you to come see her out of this life.” A low singing like a breeze. The land was starless and turned black as the throat of a mine. Then I heard Isa moaning somewhere behind me. I turned and could not tell if it was my wife or my horse in pain. On my knees I flailed, my arms reached out searching for the stone in all that impossible and coarse nothingness, a pebble in a blind wasteland. Breath fell across my face. Something combed through my hair and circled my wrists and I was pulled forward, the familiar old tune sighing above me. I called my wife’s name in a litany. By the grace of some faith I didn’t dare address the wizard’s stone smashed into my chin and lodged against my neck. My hands were released and deafness fell once more in a sharp hush over everything. I kissed that stone. I laid my head upon it and watched for the sun to break open the east. # The horse had snapped a foreleg and lay rolling its eyes. Its barrel was strangely bloated. I drove my sword into its brain and gathered what precious little gear I had, then setting my eyes upon the changed land I walked into the morning, kerchief tied to my face against the dervishes of sand. For another mound had appeared, to the north between me and the mountains, and a second rose perhaps seven miles to the west. I thought of Apache burial grounds, though they had always planted their dead in rows where tree roots could entwine their bones. Mounds were used by tribes in parts of the world I’d only heard rumors of. Yet death felt remote here in this dead place. I thought of anthills, brittle paper wasp nests, enormous worms of faintest myth. I thought of sheer scale, the speed with which these great domes were constructed. And what might have emerged from the one in El Porvenir. I thought of one foot in front of the other and eyed the desert’s split floor. My canteen had been empty since noon, my feet blistered corpses in their boots, when I came at dusk upon a small fire near the northern mound. The figure hunched before it lifted its head and turned to me. It was the blood man and his remaining eye had been taken, leaving now two hollowed and weeping sockets. “Guenard,” he said, and I sat down and regarded him. He was weak and grown ancient since I last saw him. “You have my stone, I see.” His mouth curved in a smile at his little joke. “I have it to thank,” I told him. I grasped at the cup of water he offered and drank with greed. “I would have been taken had this stone not deafened me.” The blood man laughed, gazing at nothing. “Your wisdom is what deafens you. What you hold is simply volcanic rock. A totem. There are certain properties in its depths but not it or any strength can protect you. I too possess certain greater properties and here I crouch in ruin.” “What is in those mounds?” I asked. He fetched a ragged sigh. “The new earth, swordsman. A regenesis. Spores readying to flower in us and yet we in turn are the spores. Gestating in their dark, leaching the bowels of the earth. Inseminated by matter upon which we have come to orbit.” The blood man tipped his head up and raised his arms in a gesture to the spreading blackness above us. “This is what you saw?” “The moments before birth. Bloom. And at the last, despair to be counted among their emissaries when I designed to be their foe.” “You speak as though you are of the stars.” “There is no difference, now. Not even for us holy monks raging from our temples. The world is covered. I hear your bones and they are weary. Sleep. I will keep watch over you.” He pointed to the stone as though he could see. “Keep it.” “And who will watch over you?” “I do not wish to block my ears. It seems I have become rather fond of them.” I lay down cradling the stone as one of my grandbabies. Sleep came fierce and quick. # The blood man was gone when I woke. The fire was dead embers. I sat with my legs crossed and ate the last of my strips of bison, the sinew salty upon my cracked lips. To the south what was once El Porvenir lay in its broken clay womb. I kept to the north. To the newer mound, as the thought of passage back over the mountains and homeward fell further into folly with each blind stagger. Soon I stood like a lost ant at its smooth roundish doorway. My family was both a desert and a breath away, the quiet a storm in my ears. I could never hope to reach Isa’s arms without water or sustenance or youth. Dark spread and at last I tossed the stone upon the ground. At once the dense and liquid smell of a woman clouded from within the mound. In my veins the blood answered it as petals reaching for sunrise. The singing arose in its murmur, divine Adelín and her vaqueros falling one by one before her. I looked to the west. There in the distance I saw the third mound issuing an enormous black birth. A host of limbs flexed reaching from the broken mud structure toward the sky as the sun fell behind it. A low drone filled the miles between. I sang with Isa. I sang with my children and their children. I sang with them all. “Pray you take, O Adelín, you take your rightful groom. The day I make, O Adelín, I make your rightful home.” Pooled in the sky were all the stars around that vast plughole. Some among them trailed out in a line of cold light. And I saw what I had not seen before: the shape of her and the train of her dress a thread through Creation. “Love,” my wife sang. “Love,” the single word collecting like beads on a string. I gripped the warm mouth of the mound and hoisted myself up. I slid into the wet and the dark and the beating hearts. Kelli Owen has become one of the most consistent voices in horror and thriller fiction over the past decade. Able to swing from hardcore horror, to supernatural horror, or quiet, atmospheric horror, Kelli's range is wide, and still growing. Her most recent novel, The Headless Boy , is her best work yet, combining both an oppressive atmosphere and a violent ending which delivers a rattling shock to the system. Allow her to deliver you to a liminal space of fear, paranoia, and self-deception with her tale, “Sharp Echoes.”
